NO ONE covers pupillary light reflex latency as a proxy for autonomic nervous system integrity → fall risk predictor. Neuro-ophthalmology bridge completely unowned.
thumbnail_angle: PUPIL + FLASHLIGHT + fall risk visual
audience_pain_point: Look at your eyes in a mirror. Shine your phone flashlight on one pupil. Count how fast it shrinks. That speed predicts whether youll fall this year
retention_mechanism: Pupillary light reflex is mediated by parasympathetic (CN III) → latency increases with autonomic neuropathy → slow pupil response = degraded parasympathetic tone = also degraded baroreflex = orthostatic hypotension = falls. Pupil response time >800ms (vs <400ms young adult) correlates with 2.5x fall risk. Your pupils are a window into the same autonomic system that keeps your blood pressure stable when you stand. If your pupils are slow to react your baroreflex is probably slow too.

title: The Clock Inside Your Liver That Changes How Your Meds Work After 60
hook_type: Hidden biological clock revelation
competitor_evidence: I078 covers liver metabolism decline (30% rule). I035 covers BP med+magnesium. I056 covers medicine cabinet+vit D. I084 covers stomach acid+medications. NO ONE covers the CIRCADIAN clock in liver cells (BMAL1/CLOCK genes) that changes drug metabolism by 40-60% depending on time of day. Chronopharmacology for seniors completely unowned.
thumbnail_angle: LIVER CLOCK + CIRCADIAN + medication timing visual
audience_pain_point: Youve been told to take your statin at night. But what if thats only half right? What if the clock inside your liver is more important than the clock on your wall?
retention_mechanism: Hepatic CYP450 enzymes follow circadian rhythm (BMAL1/CLOCK transcription factors) → CYP3A4 peaks at 10 PM troughs at 10 AM → drugs metabolized by CYP3A4 (statins calcium channel blockers benzodiazepines) clear 40-60% faster at night → morning dosing = higher blood levels = more side effects → evening dosing = lower levels = potentially subtherapeutic. Your liver has a clock and its not set to your schedule. Chronopharmacology: matching drug timing to enzyme peaks.

title: What Your Earwax Says About Your Heart After 60 — Doctor Explains
hook_type: Embarrassing body output + hidden organ connection
competitor_evidence: I051 covers tinnitus+brain blood flow. I077 covers hearing aid fatigue. I097 covers watery eyes. NO ONE covers earwax consistency as a biomarker. Wet vs dry earwax is determined by ABCC11 gene — same gene variant associated with apocrine gland function AND lipid metabolism. Completely novel body-signal angle.
thumbnail_angle: EARWAX + HEART + genetic visual
audience_pain_point: I know this is gross. But what if I told you the gunk in your ear could tell you something about your heart that your cholesterol test cant?
retention_mechanism: ABCC11 gene determines wet (dominant) vs dry (recessive) earwax → wet earwax = higher apocrine gland activity = higher lipid secretion = correlates with higher LDL/ApoB levels → dry earwax = lower cardiovascular risk profile. Earwax consistency change (suddenly becoming drier or wetter) can signal thyroid dysfunction or lipid metabolism shifts. Your earwax is not just wax — its a genetic biomarker sitting in your ear canal your whole life.
